data.title: Twilio Reports Better-Than-Expected Q4 Results
data.description:
• Twilio shares are consolidating. Where are TWLO shares going?

Twilio Beats Estimates In Q4

Twilio posted fourth-quarter revenue of $1.37 billion, beating analyst estimates of $1.32 billion, according to Benzinga Pro. The company reported adjusted earnings of $1.33 per share for the quarter, beating estimates of $1.24 per share.

Total revenue was up 14% year-over-year. Twilio said it had more than 402,000 active customer accounts as of Dec. 31, 2025, up from 325,000 year-over-year.

Twilio generated $271.6 million in operating cash flow and $256.1 million in free cash flow during the quarter. The company ended the period with approximately $682.34 million in cash and cash equivalents.

“We accelerated revenue growth, expanded operating margins, and delivered significant growth in free cash flow. Importantly, our vision is resonating with customers and Twilio is quickly becoming a foundational infrastructure layer in the age of AI,” said Khozema Shipchandler, CEO of Twilio.

Twilio Issues Guidance Above Estimates

Twilio expects first-quarter revenue in the range of approximately $1.34 billion to $1.35 billion versus estimates of $1.29 billion. The company anticipates first-quarter adjusted earnings of $1.21 to $1.26 per share versus estimates of $1.22 per share.

Twilio sees full-year 2026 revenue in the range of $5.65 billion to $5.70 billion versus estimates of $5.46 billion.

Twilio executives will further discuss the quarter on an earnings call with investors and analysts at 5 p.m. ET.

TWLO Stock Struggles For Direction

TWLO Price Action: Twilio shares were down 1.28% in after-hours, trading at $109.10 at the time of publication on Thursday, according to Benzinga Pro.

Image: Shutterstock.com

data.title: Arista Networks Stock Climbs After Strong Q4 Results: Details
data.description:
Arista Networks Inc. (NYSE:ANET) shares rallied in Thursday's extended trading after the company released a strong fourth-quarter earnings report and forward guidance above estimates.

Here's a look at the key figures from the quarter. 

  • ANET stock is moving. Watch the price action here.

Arista Beats Estimates

Arista reported quarterly earnings of 82 cents per share, which beat the Street estimate of 76 cents, according to data from Benzinga Pro.

Quarterly revenue clocked in at $2.49 billion, which beat the analyst consensus estimate of $2.38 billion and was up from $1.93 billion in the same period last year.

“2025 was the year of validation of our Arista 2.0 momentum, as we hit the milestone of shipping a cumulative of 150 million ports,” stated Jayshree Ullal.

“We exceeded both our AI networking and campus expansion goals, delivering profitable growth and revenue of $9 billion,” Ullal added.

Looking Ahead to Q1

For the first quarter of 2026, Arista expects:

  • Revenue of approximately $2.6 billion, versus the $2.45 billion analyst estimate.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin is between approximately 62%-63%.
  • Non-GAAP operating margin of approximately 46%.

ANET Stock Price: According to data from Benzinga Pro, Arista Networks stock rose 15.46% to $155.03 in Thursday's extended trading.  

Photo: Shutterstock

data.title: The Typical American Worker Has Less Than $1,000 Saved for Retirement — Here's How to Start Catching Up
data.description:
The median American worker has just $955 saved for retirement, according to a report issued by the National Institute on Retirement Security. That figure includes roughly 56 million workers who have no access to an employer-sponsored retirement plan.

Even among workers who do have retirement accounts, the median balance is about $40,000, far below the roughly $1.5 million many Americans say they need to retire comfortably.

For millions of workers, the problem is a lack of structure. 

Without automatic payroll savings, employer matches, or professional guidance, many people never build momentum. That is why more workers are now using services like SmartAsset to connect with financial financial advisors who can show them, in concrete terms, how far behind they are and what adjustments actually matter.

Instead of guessing, users answer a short questionnaire and are matched with up to three advisors who work with people in similar situations. Initial consultations are typically free, and there is no obligation to move forward.

The Retirement Gap Is Structural, Not Personal

The savings shortfall is not evenly distributed. More than half of private-sector workers lack access to a 401(k) or similar plan. Hispanic workers, lower-income earners, and those without college degrees are especially likely to be excluded.

If you are not saving through work, the odds are stacked against you from the start.

For workers nearing retirement, the numbers are worse. People ages 55 to 64 have accumulated only about 19% of what standard benchmarks suggest they should have saved. Fidelity recommends having eight times your annual income saved by age 60. The typical worker in that range has closer to $30,000.

That is not enough to cover even one year of expenses.

This is where personalized modeling becomes critical. Many workers assume they are behind, but do not know by how much, or which levers matter most. Advisors matched through SmartAsset can help translate abstract targets into realistic, personalized plans.

Start With What You Can Control

If you are behind, the most important step is starting, even if it feels late.

If your employer offers a 401(k) match, contributing enough to receive the full match should be the first priority. That is immediate return on your money.

If you do not have a workplace plan, opening an IRA is the next step. In 2026, workers can contribute up to $7,500, plus a $1,000 catch-up contribution for those age 50 and older.

For older workers, catch-up contributions can be powerful. In 2026, employees 50 and over can contribute up to $32,000 to a 401(k). Even modest increases compound. An extra $300 per month invested at a 7% annual return grows to roughly $52,000 over 10 years.

Whether those contributions should go into a traditional IRA, Roth account, brokerage account, or employer plan depends on taxes, income, and timing. That is another area where professional guidance can materially improve outcomes.

Why Timing Matters as Much as Saving

Saving more is only part of the equation. When you access benefits matters just as much.

Claiming Social Security at 62 instead of full retirement age reduces monthly benefits by about 30%. Waiting until 70 increases them by roughly 24%.

For workers with limited savings, that difference can determine whether retirement is stable or financially stressful. Since Social Security provides about half of income for the typical retiree, optimizing claiming strategy is often one of the highest-impact decisions.

Many workers make this choice without modeling the long-term impact. Advisors connected through SmartAsset help clients evaluate when to claim, how it interacts with withdrawals, and how it fits into overall cash flow.

Cut Big Expenses, Not Just Small Ones

On the spending side, cutting major fixed costs matters far more than trimming small discretionary items.

Housing remains the largest expense for most households. Downsizing, relocating, or paying off a mortgage before retirement can free up thousands of dollars per year. The NIRS report found that home equity represents about one-third of financial assets for working adults, leaving many people house-rich but cash-poor.

Some retirees tap that equity through downsizing or reverse mortgages. Those decisions are highly situational and often misunderstood. Reviewing them with an advisor can prevent costly mistakes.

Working Longer Still Works

Working longer is rarely appealing, but it remains one of the most effective retirement tools.

Delaying retirement by even two or three years allows savings to grow, reduces withdrawal years, and boosts Social Security benefits. According to AARP, about 7% of retirees have returned to work recently, with nearly half citing financial pressure.

As savings remain low and costs remain high, extended careers are becoming more common.

A More Realistic Target Than $1.5 Million

Most workers will not reach $1.5 million. But that does not mean retirement is unattainable.

For someone planning to spend $50,000 per year in retirement, Social Security may cover about $25,000. That leaves $25,000 to come from savings. Using a 4% withdrawal guideline, that requires roughly $625,000 saved.

It is still challenging, but it is achievable with a structured plan.

This is where many workers benefit from seeing projections side by side. SmartAsset's platform allows users to compare how different savings rates, retirement ages, and investment strategies affect long-term outcomes.

Turn Your Late Start Into a Plan

Starting late is not ideal. But it is far better than never starting.

Maximizing employer matches, using catch-up contributions, delaying Social Security, managing housing costs, and working a few extra years can collectively reshape retirement outcomes.

SmartAsset's free advisor-matching tool gives workers a way to see where they stand, what changes matter most, and how to close the gap with realistic steps. 

For millions who are behind, that personalized assessment could be the most practical starting point.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Rivian Q4 Highligths: Double Beat, R2 On Track For Q2, Delivery Guidance 62,000 To 67,000
data.description:
Electric vehicle company Rivian Automotive (NASDAQ:RIVN) reported fourth-quarter financial results Thursday after market close.

Here are the key highlights.

Rivian Q4 Results

Rivian reported fourth quarter revenue of $1.29 billion, down from $1.73 billion in last year's fourth quarter. The revenue total beat a Street consensus estimate of $1.27 billion, according to data from Benzinga Pro.

The total included $839 million in automotive revenue, down 45% year-over-year. The decline came with a drop in regulatory credit sales, lower vehicle deliveries and a lower average sales price. The quarter saw $447 million in software and services revenue, up 109% year-over-year.

Rivian reported a loss of 54 cents per share, beating a Street consensus estimate of a loss of 68 cents per share.

The company had a consolidated gross profit of $120 million in the fourth quarter and $144 million for the full fiscal year, improving this figure by $1.3 billion compared to fiscal 2024.

Previously reported production and deliveries were 10,974 vehicles and 9,745 vehicles respectively for the fourth quarter. The company delivered 42,247 vehicles for the full fiscal year.

Full-year total revenue for Rivian was $5.39 billion, up 8% year-over-year.

"In 2025 we focused on execution as we laid the foundation for dramatically scaling our business," Rivian CEO RJ Scaringe said.

What's Next for Rivian

Rivian said the R2 vehicle remains on track for first customer deliveries in the second quarter of 2026.

"It's incredibly exciting to see the early strong reviews of the R2 pre-production builds, and we can't wait to get them to our customers next quarter," Scaringe said.

Rivian provided 2026 annual guidance to deliver 62,000 to 67,000 vehicles in 2026, up significantly from 2025.

The company sees adjusted EBITDA in a range of a loss of $2.1 billion to a loss of $1.80 billion for fiscal 2026, with capital expenditures of $1.95 billion to $2.05 billion for the full fiscal year.

Rivian also said it will provide additional product details on March 12, which include line-up details.

Rivian Stock Price Action

Rivian stock is up 16.6% to $16.30 in after-hours Thursday versus a 52-week trading range of $10.36 to $22.69.

Photo: Shutterstock

data.title: AI Disruption Fears Slam Real Estate Brokers
data.description:
CBRE Group Inc (NYSE:CBRE), Jones Lang LaSalle Inc (NYSE:JLL) and Cushman & Wakefield Ltd (NYSE:CWK) closed lower Thursday as investors continued to reassess whether fast-improving artificial intelligence tools can chip away at the commercial real estate services model.

A Bloomberg report on Wednesday framed the move as part of an "AI scare trade," with the sector selling off as markets priced in the risk that AI could disrupt labor-heavy, fee-based professional services.

  • CBRE Group stock is showing notable weakness. Why are CBRE shares down?

What These Companies Actually Do

CBRE Group, Jones Lang LaSalle and Cushman & Wakefield are all "picks-and-shovels" firms for commercial property — office, industrial, retail and multifamily — earning fees for advising on and executing complex transactions and ongoing operations.

Those revenue streams are cyclical, tied to transaction volumes and historically justified by information advantages, negotiation expertise and process management.

Why This Matters: The AI Disintermediation Question

If those pieces take less time and fewer hands, large real estate service platforms may eventually have to rethink headcount — and could face downward pressure on pricing for routine work.

Market moves this week suggest some traders are already discounting businesses that rely heavily on large teams and high-margin advisory fees.

CBRE Earnings Beats, Guidance Midpoint Tops Estimates

CBRE Group drew early attention Thursday after reporting a better-than-expected fourth-quarter adjusted EPS of $2.73, topping the $2.67 consensus estimate, even as revenue of about $11.63 billion came in slightly below expectations of roughly $11.71 billion.

The company also issued FY2026 adjusted EPS guidance of $7.30 to $7.60, with the midpoint above Wall Street estimates, helping support the stock earlier in the session before reversing lower and closing around 8% lower to $136.28 Thursday.

Zillow Earnings: A Side read-through, Not The Main Driver

Commentary also pointed to rising legal costs and uncertainty tied to private listing networks as near-term overhangs.

Price action: Wednesday Through Thursday

Image: Shutterstock
